sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 45-year-old male, presented with an ankle injury sustained while playing basketball with his 10-year-old son.
He described landing awkwardly after being tackled and twisting his ankle, resulting in bruising and swelling on the outside of the ankle.
He reports difficulty walking and instability, which he attributes to the ankle not feeling solid or stable.
He had been in a good workout routine and is concerned about disrupting this momentum.
Past medical history: [NOT MENTIONED]
Review of systems: [NOT MENTIONED]
Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam: The patient's ankle is swollen on the lateral side, with exquisite tenderness to palpation laterally and some tenderness over the medial deltoid region.
The skin is intact, and capillary refill is brisk.
There is no malalignment.
The patient reports pain when standing and walking on the ankle.
Test Results: X-ray of the right ankle, AP lateral oblique, shows a displaced lateral malleolus fracture at the Weber C level, with no evidence of medial or posterior malleolar fractures.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has a displaced lateral malleolar fracture of the right ankle.
Problem list: Lateral malleolar fracture; active.
Plan:
I prescribed meloxicam 7.5 mg orally every 8 hours for pain and inflammation.
I advised the patient to ice the ankle and keep it elevated.
I recommended that the patient use crutches and avoid putting weight on the ankle until further notice.
I informed the patient that he will need surgery to stabilize the fracture, which will involve the placement of a plate and screws, and that he will be out for approximately three months.
I encouraged the patient to continue coaching his son's basketball team in April.
Follow-up: The patient will follow up with me in three months to assess the healing of the fracture.
